description: This is "2026-05-08 09-34-49" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.

chunks.text: Thank you very much, everybody, for your attendance today. We are now starting the formal part of the day with regards to our role as the Māori Affairs Select Committee of Parliament itself. But first of all, thank you very much for the mean pocket time. It's a very warm welcome to such a beautiful place that you have here itself. And one of the treasures that we have as a committee is to be able to get around the country and come to places to use when you are and to enjoy your hospitality, but also to see sometimes places that we've never been before. And that's myself on this occasion,

this is the first time I've had the opportunity to be here and I can tell you. I was excited to come and I'm pleased that I have come. So thank you for the very warm welcome to your pinua here itself. So today the process here is that we as a select committee, we are here to hear from those who submitted to the process of this Treaty settlement that we have in front of us here. It is usual for us to hear those that are in support of the Treaty settlement, but it's not unusual for us to hear from people that actually are not in support of the Treaty settlement for whatever reason they may have. So today is about us gaining a better understanding of what the submissions are that are coming towards us to take into account when we deliberate on behalf

of the settlement. Clearly there's been a long, you might argue, arduous timeframe of actually getting it to this point here, but nevertheless this particular stage is an important one for the Crown because what we are talking about here is creating law and law binds everybody towards it, including the Crown. And I take words of some of the submissions here. The settlement itself is the beginning of a rebuild of an area of different places that we intend itself. So we are very keen to hear from the submitters. Today we are looking at hearing from five parties

today. It's our convention that we always start with the negotiating group to open up these the proceedings, but we also, as for Fairness, is to allow them to close up at some time at the end as well. That's what we've always done as a committee and we feel that that is a fair way. We will also be having a hearing in Wellington also. So there are some people that couldn't make their way to here today and for Fairness we want to make sure they have the opportunity also. So we will be conducting a hearing in Wellington in a short time as well. So the proceedings here, our convention, like everything is largely convention with the regards to sleep committees, is that we generally give groups 10 minutes to present

and individual five minutes to present and we'd like to allow a bit of time for some questions as well within those time frames. We've only got a short number today so as the chair, I'll be a little bit flexible but we do have still have time to get by and please excuse me if I have to interrupt and move on to questions or to close the submission off at that time so I don't want to mind. Now we have a number of people that are present with us today that are from Wellington and other places as well and of course we heard from Martia who spoke earlier on and Martia is here to help us with translation. Not all of us are fluent in terms of some of us are but not all of us are so it's very important that we understand particularly if you wish to use Tariel in your presentation and that's

absolutely accepted. So we have a number of other staff from Te Tari Pakatoa, the Ministry of Trans-Sentiments but also from our Parliamentary Services staff who help us out as a committee to make sure. There's a number of formalities here it is a recorded meeting so we record the meeting it's not zoomed is it? It's actually just a recording today obviously usually we would actually have it publicly available. This is a public meeting so people are able to are allowed to come in here unless I feel that an individual or a grouping are causing disruption to our process and not adding constructively to it then I have a role as chair to manage that if they've never had to do that no if I don't have to do that today and it's also

convenient you'll see that we've got a couple of helpers here with Henare and also Jeff for Security that's normal for us. Parliamentary Services who have a responsibility of looking after the welfare of us all but including the members of Parliament we just need to make sure that we've got ourselves being kept safe as well. Our staff came here yesterday and they apparently they moved these tables around a number of different ways to try and get the best positioning to be able to conduct this so thank you to the staff for the work you've done and I'm sure it will work fine. So with that let me make sure there's nothing else I need to say I think I've covered most of it itself thank you once again we look very much forward to

hearing what you have and first up as I said before we've got a Te Pote Taupoki who has been doing the negotiations and all that and we'll open with your submission to begin with Te Arawa. Akiwata. So I'm submitting on behalf of the Te Pote Taupoki which is a trust established to receive the Dean of Settlement on behalf of Nati Uppani Maiwai. Nati Uppani Maiwai want to settle and trust strong support of the Nati Uppani Maiwai that he want to claim settlement bill

and urge the Justice Committee to recommend that it's passed with an amendment. This settlement has been a long time coming going on 40 years now people have waited generations for the Crown to acknowledge what the Crown did to our people. The Dean of Settlement represents a hard one negotiated agreement made in good faith and the bill gives it legal effect. We ask Parliament to honour that agreement. Trust was established specifically to receive the settlement and all its benefits

in trust current and future generations of Nati Uppani Maiwai Karamon. We are ready our governance is in place and our pathway towards it is prepared and our people are waiting. The historical account in the Dean records plainly and permanently the serious Crown wrongs committed against Nati Uppani Maiwai Karamon. These include the home Crown attacks on the Kōpani and Onepoto in the 1860s and the scorched earth tactics that destroyed our Kaimai Free Supply. Coercive and unlawful lands and dealings that strip Nati Uppani Maiwai Karamon

lands under pressure and duress. Broken promises of self-government under the Udo Wera Reserve arrangements and decades of dispossession that left our people virtually landless and severed from our ancestral rule. Our ancestral rule. The Crown's formal acknowledgments and apology in the Dean recognises these breaches of the tenacity of Waitangi and the profound harms they caused. Lots of life, lots of land, cultural dislocation and long-term socioeconomic damage across generations. There is not simply illegal formality. The public record and apology are vital for that for our people.

They validate the limited experience of our kōmatsu and confirm the truth of what happened. They acknowledge the intergenerational harm trauma that continues to affect our whānau today. They provide a foundation for restoring honour and building a just ongoing relationship between the Crown and Nati Uppani Maiwai Karamon. Most importantly this history told and recorded by us becomes a permanent record and Mokopunna will always know who they are, where they come from and why this redress matters.

Cultural redress in this city is significant and is important that we expect unique. Transfer of lands into Te Udo Wera reflects a clear and principled shift away from the Crown's model of private ownership toward a framework that recognises Te Udo Wera as a legal entity in its own right and affirms Nati Uppani Maiwai Karamon's role as Kaitiaki which has always been a truth. Our mana and Kaitiaki tam over this law here were never extinguished. They were suppressed by Crown actions. The Te Udo Wera Act model corrects that. Whakapapa becomes the legal foundation for access, use and responsibility.

Entirely consistent with our Te Uppuna, understood and exercised authority over this law here since time immemorial. Settlement also transfers specific sites including Tūriokahu and Kaitawa and its Crown properties into Te Udo Wera. These sites provide places for housing and reconnection to our whānau, governance arrangements, embed our decision-making and tikanga alongside statutory protections enabling conservation, visitor use and community needs to be managed together in a way that reflects our values.

In short, the settlement gives legal expression to what has always been true that Nati Uppani Maiwai Karamon has a hapu of two ways of holding responsibilities of Kaitiaki Tam within Te Udo Wera. It creates a durable framework for that authority to be exercised, recognised and honoured into the future. Nati Uppani Maiwai Karamon has a hapu of two ways. That Whakapapa relationship was not administrative, it is who we are. It defines our identity, our obligations to this law and the tikanga under which we exercise our Kaitiaki Tam within Te Udo Wera. Our relationship with two ways is one of

whanau and is from that foundation that we act as Thamata Whanaua of Waikaremona. Two ways of supporting the settlement, that support is not incidental, it reflects a shared Whakapapa understanding of the responsibilities and authority that belongs to the people of this law. It is that living relationship, not administrative structures that will continue to guide how we care for Waikaremona and Te Udo Wera. As required by the treaty settlement process, we engage with all neighbouring iwi throughout the negotiation of this settlement. Those conversations were conducted in good faith and are a matter of record. Whether we have raised concerns about

the settlement, we acknowledge those views while respectfully disagreeing. Our authority over this law is grounded in Whakapapa and in the continuity of our presence and responsibilities as Ahikaa. There is not a position we arrive at through negotiation, there is not one we will depart. Other iwi have had the opportunity through the treaty settlement process to achieve redress to their claims. We do not accept that our settlement is the appropriate vehicle from re-litigating their outcomes. Financial and commercial redress, the trust acknowledges honestly that no financial

settlement can fully compensate for the scale of what was taken from Nazi Europe and the losses of land and of economic capacity across generations cannot be reduced to a dollar figure. What the financial and commercial redress does provide is a foundation. It gives us the tools to begin rebuilding the economic strength and prosperity our people have had before being disenfranchised from our land and customary rights. The trust is prepared to deploy these assets purposefully, to grow them into jobs, services and intergenerational wealth for our people. Partners have been identified, plans are in place and we will

report transparently on outcomes to our youth. The settlement does not end our journey and begins the rebuilding. We wish to speak plainly to the committee about what continued delay means for our people. We have carried this hurt for too long. The weight of unresolved historical grievance has worn down our values, our beliefs, our relationships with one another. Reaching in action brings more disconnection from our role, more lost opportunities to invest in our far known Langata, our deeper harm to the way well of our community. Our co-martial have waited their whole lives and many have passed the lead now.

Whole lives from this acknowledgement, our Langata deserves to inherit our settlement, empowered identity and not a legacy of unresolved grievance. The bill is the first essential step toward the restoration. Passing it now means we can begin the real work. Healing relationships, restoring kaitiakitanga over Waikato Mauna, strengthening our far known well-being and building a stable future. Further delay will deepen the harm. There is no benefit for the crown or to our people in prolonging the process. The Ngati Rupani Maewaikato Mauna settlement first respectfully requests that the Justice Committee recommend that the Ngati Rupani

Maewaikato Mauna claim settlement bill be passed without a means. Acknowledging its report, the significance of historical record and crown apology to the people of Ngati Rupani Maewaikato. Prioritizing the bill's passage said that the work of restoration and rebuilding can begin without questions. I will take a couple of short questions now, but understanding we'll have another opportunity at the end of our hearing to ask further questions, but I think we already have questions that we have from members at this time. Are you asking a question? Yes. Unfortunately, the process is here for us to ask questions,

we don't turn it across examination. I mean to sort of pick apart one of the other submissions? She's part of the other submission. Yeah, so you get an opportunity to talk to what you wish to then, okay? So, thank you very much, Kara, for that verbal submission itself and we'll be seeing you again later on. So, thank you. Kia ora. Kia ora. So, we'll move now to Ngati Rupani Maewaikato Mauna Koa and Dr. Rapa Tawhiti. Rapa Tawhiti, 10 minutes out today. So, you use it how you see, but we have the opportunity

to read the written submission, but you use it as you wish to. My name is Dr. Rapa Tawhiti and I speak on behalf of Ngati Rupani Kiwita Kitae Cooperative Society. I am one of seven directors. We were set up under the Companies Act to Unify All Ngati Rupani from Waikarewana to Waiiwau to Gisborne because our Te Puna Rupani is actually from Gisborne. The current submitters Te Whare Taupuki represent Ngati Rupani Maewaikarewana, affectionately referred to by Alston Best in his book Two Way As Children of the Mist.

First, we represent Ngati Rupani Kiwika Rupana who are effectively the children who got mistyped. So, the difference between Mai Waikarewana and Ki Waikarewana is fundamental to this proposed bill. First, we vehemently oppose the proposed bill passed on the fact that its foundations, its framework, are seriously flawed. In particular, I draw your attention to Te Whare Taupuki's submission which states that Ngati Rupani is a hapu of Two Way. We have proven beyond all

reasonable doubt in the Waitangi Tribunal hearing of 2004 that Ngati Rupani are not a hapu of Two Way. Rupani, this large year was named after Rupani as a male ancestor from Gisborne who came here, his six generations from the Whaka Horohta which landed in Gisborne. Two Way comes from another waka called Matatua. Two Way comes from Whakatane. So, there is no Whakapapa connection between Rua Pani and Two Way. So, from the outset, the submission by Te Whare Taupuki is flawed in terms of its own Whakapapa.

To clarify, there are two entities at the core of this debate. This proposal only caters for those for Rua Pani who Whakapapa to Two Way and does not cater for those for Rua Pani who Whakapapa to Kahungunu to all the land in between here in Waialu to Gisborne and who have land rights and the six plots that have been transferred to Te Urewera Board, which is a Two Way Board. That's what's proposed under this bill. Therefore, this bill must be urgently halted. This bill is inconsistent with the Crown's Treaty

obligations of active protection and good faith. The Whakapapa in history of the submittance to Te Whare Taupuki who have just discovered are the PSGE. We had no idea. They're going to receive this $24 million plus the six plots of land. It's incorrect and it is Two Way centric. By passing this bill, you are validating false Whakapapa and a fabricated history of Ngati Rua Pani in my Waikarewana, which is essentially a Two Way biased history. Even the Māori translation of this report is Two Way biased. It's written in the

Two Way dialect. We oppose the bill vehemently as we argue that false Whakapapa and false historical accounts cannot be validated by the Crown or its agencies or by its ministers. In their Whakapapa and historical accounts, which are documented in this settlement, the history of Rua Pani, Ngati Rua Pani is confused and influenced by an ideology called Te Mana Motuhake o Two Way, which states believes that Ngati Rua Pani is a haku of Two Way, four to long side Two Way, to define this beautiful Te Uruweta Genohi Portae o Two Way,

the Uruweta District Native Reserve in 1896. However, if you read their historical account, the history that they have written fails to acknowledge that Ngati Rua Pani withdrew from Te Rua Portae o Two Way in 1899. We withdrew. And we formed our own Mana Motuhake as Rua Pani in 1899 and again in 1917 with the Uruweta Consolidation Scheme. When we formed the reserves, the 12 reserves up at Lake Waikaremoana are only for Ngati Rua Pani, Kewaikaremoana, Tuturu, Ngati Rua Pani, 317 Ngati Rua Pani

owners. We have a list of owners. Well, I have argued before the Māori land court last year when the submitters, the son, the grave diger, or Pohene this morning, said that the meeting house up to the land, the Maraea people, owns the Two Way. Well, I objected to that based on the fact that Te Mana Motuhake or Two Way is nothing more than ideological Mambo-Jamba. Therefore, the Whakapapa and historical account presented by Te Whare Taupuki must not be validated by the select committee. I ask the select committee to pause. By all, we impose the transfer of the six blocks to Te Uruweta Board

and argue that this bill contradicts te kanga Māori. And I refer to case law. Case law, the Supreme Court, the case of Pakinikura and Te Kauri Hira Kau Matua or Two Way versus Te Uitou Matua in 2023, where the Supreme Court ruled that te kanga cannot be its own judge. This is a Supreme Court decision. Therefore, this cannot, breach of te kanga cannot be validated by this select committee. This bill is also a breach of Tenoranatiratone guaranteed in Article 2 of the Treaty. And Mana Motuhake, which Mr. Trainer Teitonau, my uncle,

argued before the constitutional kōpapa inquiry in Palmerston North last year, the draft report. You can read our evidence at pages 31 and 32 of the draft report of the constitutional kōpapa inquiry where we rejected the constitutional legitimacy of unilateral crown sovereignty, which allows laws such as this proposed bill to be passed without consultation. This proposed bill creates enduring legal prejudice. We also oppose the bill because the Crown has relied on one group's mandate,

Ngāti Rua Pani mai Wa Karemoana, the children of the mist. The Crown has failed to properly resolve overlapping claims with our whānau, with our hapū and the six blocks that have been transferred to Te Uruwera Bo. This is inconsistent with tribunal guidance that overlapping interests must be properly addressed, not overwritten or ignored as some sort of inter-tribal dispute. In their submission to Whare Taupoki, claims that the history is plain and permanent. However, we argue that the history is not as plain and permanent as simple as they might

lead you to believe. The historical account they have provided is very much an unsettled history. The historical account is Tuhoe centric. Tuhoe centric. Tuhoe bias treats Ngāti Rua Pani as a hapū. We are not a hapū or tuhoe. We are an iwi in our own right. The historical account has not been agreed upon by all affected parties. You've heard, I've read the submissions from our cousins from Ngāti Kahungunu, Tātau Tātau. Kāroha, kāroha. We have one here. He's going to give evidence today. This is a dangerous precedent. At risk of becoming legislated history,

this creates a dangerous precedent because disputed historical narratives become permanently embedded in law. I believe in the rule of law. I gained a master of laws from Victoria University. I don't believe in the law of the jungle. I believe in the rule of law. We are possible because Te Uru Wera Board and land authority issues. There are issues with the Board. The bill embeds redress within Te Uru Wera framework, effectively reinforcing Tuhoe governance structures, limits recognition of other iwi authorities and PSTEs such as Tātau Tātau.

The locks are fabricated to protection of mana whenua and that is based on lies and the manipulation of whakapapa. Dr. Winnie, could you just point out any new outcomes or cover a little bit of what you said about it? We've been denied equal recognition. We're structurally excluded. The prejudice is severe and irreversible and the bill will cause permanent loss of mana whenua, loss of treaty claim rights, distortion of whakapapa and entrenchment of incorrect historical narratives. If I may just

talk briefly about delay. Very briefly. Te Whare Tau Toki argues that delay will cause us harm. We oppose this because we believe that a rust settlement will create a flawed settlement and a flawed settlement will cause far greater harm than any delay. Recommendations and I close up here. We respectfully requested this bill will not and cannot proceed in its current form. We requested an independent review of whakapapa assertions historical account mandate and agreement in principle. We request genuine engagement with Ngāti Rua Pani ki Waikarewana claimants and community as well as Tato Tato and other affected PSTEs. We respectfully urge the

select committee to delay and pause the progress of the bill until these concerns have been independently examined and resolved. A treaty settlement founded upon unresolved grievances cannot deliver true and enduring justice. We are not opposed to treaty settlements, but we urge the select committee to pause on passing this bill in order to make the foundations of this treaty settlement more solid, more robust, more transparent and fairer for everyone to consider.

You would know from the legal process around these settlements that the effect of the Māori Affairs Select Committee's responsibility is to formalize a data settlement that's been already signed and traditionally it's peculiar or unusual to delay

so you will make the case for exceptional circumstances. Obviously there's often disagreement with any way about whether a settlement should be made, whether it's sufficient and we understand we're acutely aware at least that I feel acutely able to visit the character of settlements often. It's one of the most I've wanted to visit the settlement processes. I wonder if understanding your profoundly expressed concerns, do you think there's a version of meaningful amendments that could be made that would resolve

them or is it really the fundamental character of it which places Māori Pāne as a part of Tūhoi when you believe that Rūpani should be as an independent Tūhoi? The settlement should be separate from any association with the Tūwhera. So I feel like I've understood that was really a clarification if I understand. I think you may have a quick answer. Thank you. You mentioned the requirement for clarification of Whakapapa and I think we can all agree that

it's not the role of the Crown or this Select Committee. How do you envisage that happening with that process and who might that group be? I think there needs to be meaningful engagement between Ngāti Rua Pāne, Māia Waikaremoana or Te Whare Taupoki and Ngāti Rua Pāne, Ki Waikaremoana and Tātou Tātou to address those overlapping issues of Whakapapa. They haven't been mentally discussed and Iroha will talk more about that, that kōrero hasn't occurred. So you need a period of time, you're requesting a period of time for an independent process driven by respective hapū iwi. What might that time frame be? Six months.

Any last questions of? Kia ora, doctor. Thank you very much for your bashing submission. We appreciate it and I think you've given us a lot of thought to be considered in our deliberations. Okay, can we now move to Tāhurio Te Rangi? We actually have you down for five minutes as an individual, but like I said before, I'll be a little bit fixable, but we've had the opportunity to read your written submission,

so you point out the key points you want us to understand. Kia ora mai. Kia ora, I don't think it's going to last long. I'm going to pass over. I'll position to the bill. I'm sorry, I've written everything in the language. This has nothing to do with the spirit of the Uruera,

nor with the thoughts of the leader, the leader of the Pakeha. I am a descendant of this country. I am very concerned about the future of the country. The decisions for the Uruera should be made to the people who are in charge of it. It is not right to take over the power of the Crown. This is a big issue. There are no representatives for the Wairoa region on the board of the Uruera.

There is no language. There are no connections, there are no words, there is no us. It is not right to change the constitution. The constitution, and if it is not to correct this issue. The Treaty of Waitangi. This is also related to the Treaty of Waitangi. The leader, the leader of the people, the leader of the land. I say that this bill is not being seen.

In our community, there are still the former leaders. When the power was raised, we did not hear it. We are still seeing these kinds of things in this bill. I wanted to. So the committee does not agree with this bill. But the Wairoa region is a representative. The power is not to go to the land. The Treaty of Waitangi is a good thing.

I will end this. The Uruera is not the river. It is a tribe. We are the people. The government is our leader to take care of it. I do not agree with its mana. I will end my speech. If the Uruera is not agreed with, we will also agree with it. We do not agree with that. So thank you. Thank you. I will just go to Kushla for a question. Thank you.

Can you hear me? Yes, I can. Thank you for talking to us this morning. If there is another member of the Wairoa on this board, if there is another member of the Wairoa on the table, can you support this bill? Yes, they are very close to us. We are here in Waitangi. We are on the Wairoa. We do not want to stop them. We want to go to others. So if there is another member of the Wairoa on the board, can you support the bill? Yes, if that is the case, we will be able to do it. Thank you.

Thank you for the questions from the members. You have given us a very good written submission as well as your verbal submission. Thank you. I think it is reiterating what your whanaua has submitted as well. Kia ora, my, for your submission today. Kia ora. I am the Marriver. Kia ora. Craig Atkinson. Welcome Craig. I had a chat with Craig earlier on. He has traveled over two hours to be with us today. So, Kia ora my Craig for making the effort to come here and you've contributed to many treaty sentiments and I'm appreciative of that but you understand

the process here. We've got five minutes allocated for your time. So, I don't want to... First of all, I'd just like to acknowledge from the mountains, the hills, the lungs, the rivers, the streams that make the magnificent place that we sit here today. I'd like to acknowledge all the people that have gone before, the haggie persecution and the tyranny from the crown, all the people that have been left to fight for years and years and years in the process, there's not fit process, a process that divides Maori and that. I'd like to acknowledge all the people that go after all the young people as we see running around and this is what these treaty claims, I believe, is about, was left for Maori and that to end poverty, to end homelessness and that there's so much we could achieve from these treaty claims and so much we fail.

And of the basis of it, the crown divides the local iwi's, hapus, they have them arguing amongst themselves that they can't come to an agreement because it's not full process. That's part of your game, you sit there and sort of smile and say, oh yeah, but it's it's yourselves that do it. You rub a stamp in, you're all under contract, you're all paid very well by the crown and that, so there is no neutrality here. There is numerous conflicts of interest by the committee and the process and the figure that you come up with, 0.007%, they get anything that's under a 0.007% or is a negative in my view. The land is never commercially

feasible, the only land they ever give back is actually what will cost the crown money, so it's just balancing its book by returning, which is a token gesture. The apology is just not, I acknowledge him and apologies not worth nothing, he's got no value whatsoever. So yeah, the entire process is flawed for Maori, at the end of it there is no natural justice and it's all set to divide Maori and things. We've just seen farmers have a payout that have a 400,000 per household, handed out, how much can we say each iwi here, each individual person, how much is this treaty claim going to put in their family, I'd say zero, none of them will ever see any

of it. So for that point it doesn't empower Maori and it does nothing more than just put that this is just another breach of what the treaty was actually set out in the beginning. The other alternate to the headline negotiation says return off land, pay the rent then you are on it, pay the final test and that, take the prison sentence for the land that's stolen, because that's what would happen if Maori took anything. If there was one acre of land taken by Maori that would be returned and that back, there would be a financial burden and there'd be a prison sentence, there's none of that either, it's one rule for one and a different rule for others and it's been like that from dear God. So that's my submission, I fully support the doctors,

submission. Thank you. Sure, thank you very much. Any questions for Craig? You stay. Good question Craig. I think a lot of us feel the truth in what you said about the hypocrisy of the Crown and how the Crown still holds the rulebook and determines the conditions on the settlement. But I want to ask you one thing is when we stand in the house and acknowledge the apology and recognise the wrongs that are now exited in black and white law

and in unanimity the members of the Parliament will stand and accept the historical account, accept the atrocities committed by the Crown and recognise the truth of that history. I can assure you that we do it genuinely and that it's not in any way to diminish this massive flaws in the Crown's settlement process. But that apology I believe is given good faith and that acknowledgement from us as members of Parliament is genuine.

You're all under contract to the Crown, you're all put by the Crown. Your first point of the contract is to protect the Crown's assets. That's where it comes from though Mike, at the end of it, 28, 24 million is an insult. Any last questions of Craig? Craig, thank you very much. Okay, now we have the written submission of Ida, Ida, thank you, Ida, Ida, and I believe you are, are you speaking on behalf of me?

Or are you either? I'm sorry, I've just got a message that it might be James that was going to be my presentation. Welcome, welcome today. And once again we've had that opportunity to read your written submission and we've got approximately five minutes out of the time. So, and here's it as you see that. Thank you. Just acknowledgement for all of you coming in today in person. This is a submission on behalf of Alphana really, submitted by my father James. He can't be seen as apologies, can't make it through today. Apologies from my two Akana, James, who can't make it through today. So,

I also live in Wairau. So, and for full transparency to acknowledge some of the koro, particularly from Rahata. I also sit as a director at Tato-Tato-Tawairo Trust and I have been at the koro that was had with Tato-Tato and Paritopoki, Ngati Rupani, my Waikaremona Trust. We're in the submission here. It's at the heart of the submission is the issue of overlapping interests. If interest is the right word,

we've talked about negotiations, responsibilities. And so, this is coming from our Hini Manuhiti Whakapapa, but in Alphano through my grandmother Frances. We whakapapa to Rupani, we whakapapa to Ngai Tuwhai, and we whakapapa to Hini Manuhiti. Hini Manuhiti being Ngati Kahungunu, to Wairau. So, Hini Manuhiti, the granddaughter of Kahungunu, Hini Manuhirikia Pukaru, the son of Rupani. So, that's really, this is winding back

several hundred years now, you know. And so, we're at the Whakapapa and for Alphano, yeah, two feet and three cams, you know. So, there was a map attached, I don't know if it came through so, the map was really speaking to that overlapping interest for a little again with the, and these are historical traditional boundaries that again go back hundreds of years. More recently and more recent history, these boundaries were talked about in countless hearings and court cases and legislation processes dating back 1875, I think one of the earliest in the wider hearing,

where the these Kahungunu, these Hini Manuhiti boundaries were put on the table. They've been disputed, they've been debated, they've been accepted and negated over all those years since 1875, Taino Kiteniwa, you know, to this day. But it just is to really just show how these, how significant these, the Spinoa is, Putanoi Waipari Moana, specifically those six dock reserves, which sit on the wider side of the lake, you know, on the southern side, and there's a, so they sit on the wider side of the lake, so, and those are where you see those overlapping,

If I just make some closing comments for myself. You know, I often look at these Treaty settlement processes in the part that we play. We, as members of Parliament, are here for a small amount of time to try and do the best we can in what I call a process that's perfectly imperfect. It's by no means, and as we've heard, I empathise with people saying that the redress is a stretch of the surface. It's a pittance of actually the wrong doings of the past. And we see this all over the water, all over the place, back where I'm from, the Karanaki.

We've seen it all there. And I'm very mindful personally that this process here is about providing a new beginning to rebuild from everything that has been lost, and that presents its challenges in itself. And the resource that comes from Treaty settlements can be easily argued as just a pittance of what it takes to begin that journey. But a bigger concern of mine is we often, if not always, hear from Rano Nui of a settlement where there's unrest between your people. And it concerns me that the Karanaki is endeavouring to start again with its responsibilities

and allow iwi to rebuild. But are we sometimes causing a new problem within iwi? I'm very mindful of that, and I ask that question about this, and I accept the journey, the arduous journey that's been had to get your settlement to this point. We play but a part in this process, and it's always valuable for us to be number one on the whenua. And I take your point, Waititi, about Kōrero being here on your whenua, and we have to go back to Wellington to discuss the query, to ask questions of people to try

and help you to start again. And that is a challenge, and it's like I said, it's imperfect where we're here. But it's so important for us to hear from everybody, your whanaua, and the different points of view, because you don't get that from reading documents. It's so important to hear and to see and to feel the empathy, feel the emotion of what this is on both sides, because we have a job here that is so important. A treaty settlement is an important part in your history. It will be looked back on in this process here, but it's just a point of time.

It's actually the future and what you're doing intergenerational for everybody else in the future, that we are mindful of trying to giving you a pathway, giving you some more open doors to be able to begin that new journey. And it doesn't come without its challenges and emotions at our end, because I think we all here reflect on our own home, Iwi and Appu, with what they've gone through in there. So it is quite an emotional journey that I say personally for us to actually go through this here. But in closing, thank you for the openness of everybody that submitted today.

On both sides of the argument, it's a thank you for being respectful to run another.
